| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Standard |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Tangential (inlet) velocity** | $v_i = Q / (B \cdot H)$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow |
| 2 | **Centrifugal (radial) acceleration** | $a_r = v_\theta^2 \,/\, r$ | Replaces gravity $g$ |
| 3 | **Equivalent diameter of inlet** | $D_e = 4BH \,/\, [2(H+B)]$ | Hydraulic diameter |
| 4 | **Number of effective turns (N$_\text{e}$)** | $N_e = \dfrac{1}{H}\left(L_b + \dfrac{L}{2}\right)$ | Lapple's geometrical factor |
| 5 | **Critical particle diameter ($d_{pc}$)** | $d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \,\mu\, W}{2 \,\pi\, N_e \, v_i \,(\rho_p - \rho)}}$ | Lapple model |
| 6 | **Terminal settling velocity ($v_{ts}$)** | $v_{ts} = \dfrac{g(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes regime ($Re < 0.1$) |
| 7 | **Single-particle grade efficiency** | $\eta(d_p) = \dfrac{1}{1 + (d_{pc}/d_p)^2}$ | Lapple (1951) form |
| 8 | **Overall fractional efficiency (Lapple)** | $\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left[-2\,(C\psi)^{-1.08}\,(d_p/d_{pc})^2\right]$ | $\psi$ = inertial parameter |
| 9 | **Stairmand pressure drop** | $\Delta P = \xi \, \dfrac{v_i^2 \, \rho}{2}$ | $\xi \approx 16$ (high-eff.) |
| 10 | **Shepherd–Lapple pressure drop** | $\Delta P = 7.5 \, \dfrac{v_i^2 \, \rho}{2}$ | Empirical; simple form |
| 11 | **Stokes number ($Stk$)** | $Stk = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v}{18 \,\mu \, D}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | **Cyclone efficiency index** | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \,\mu \, B}{\pi \, N_e \, v_i \, \rho_p}}$ | 50 % cut diameter |
| 13 | **Power consumed** | $W_p = Q \cdot \Delta P / \eta_{\text{fan}}$ | Fan efficiency $\approx 0.6$ |

> **Notation Convention Used:** $\mu$ = gas viscosity (Pa·s); $\rho$ = gas density; $\rho_p$ = particle density; $W$ = inlet width $B$ (Lapple's original notation); $C$ = Cunningham slip correction (often $\approx 1$ for $d_p > 1 \mu m$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cement kilns** use large-diameter (≥ 2 m) cyclones as **pre-heater pre-cleaners**, removing 70 – 90 % of coarse dust before ESPs.
> - **Boiler houses** in the power sector rely on multi-cyclones downstream of fluidized bed combustors.
> - **Wood & grain processing** facilities use cyclones to recover product dust and prevent fire/explosion hazards.
> - **Pulp & paper** mills use cyclones as **separators** (not collectors) to remove shives from black liquor.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Particle Diameter (Lapple Model)

The **critical particle diameter** $d_{pc}$ is the diameter of that particle which, under the action of the centrifugal field, will *just* reach the cyclone wall before being carried out by the gas. It defines the **theoretical boundary of separation**.

### Step 1 — Particle Radial Velocity

Inside the cyclone, the radial drift velocity of a particle (under Stokes' drag law) is given by balancing centrifugal force with drag:

$$ v_r = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_\theta^2}{18 \,\mu \, r} $$

*Reasoning:* Force balance gives $3 \pi \mu d_p v_r = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6}(\rho_p - \rho) \dfrac{v_\theta^2}{r}$. Solving yields the radial drift.

### Step 2 — Particle Residence Time in the Vortex

For a particle to be collected, it must reach the wall (radius $R = D/2$) while the gas makes one effective turn. The radial travel time is:

$$ t_r = \int_{r}^{R} \frac{dr}{v_r} $$

Substituting the expression for $v_r$:

$$ t_r = \frac{18 \,\mu}{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_\theta^2} \int_{r}^{R} r \, dr $$

$$ t_r = \frac{9 \,\mu \, (R^2 - r^2)}{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_\theta^2} $$

### Step 3 — Gas Residence Time per Effective Turn

The gas travels along a helical path. Per *effective turn* ($N_e$), the gas traverses an axial length equal to $L_b + L/2$ at velocity $v_i$. The residence time per turn is:

$$ t_g = \frac{L_b + L/2}{v_i} = \frac{N_e \, H}{v_i} $$

*Reasoning:* Each helical turn has pitch $H$ (one inlet height), so for $N_e$ turns the axial travel is $N_e H$. The total time spent in $N_e$ turns equals $(N_e H)/v_i$.

### Step 4 — Equating Residence Times (Collection Criterion)

For the *critical* particle ($d_p = d_{pc}$), the particle must just reach the wall in time $t_g$. Setting $t_r = t_g$ and approximating $r \approx 0$ (particle starts near the center for maximum travel):

$$ \frac{9 \,\mu \, R^2}{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_{pc}^2 \, v_\theta^2} = \frac{N_e \, H}{v_i} $$

### Step 5 — Using Rankine Vortex Approximation

In the **forced vortex** region (near the wall), $v_\theta = v_i$, and $R = D/2$. Substituting and solving for $d_{pc}$:

$$ d_{pc}^2 = \frac{9 \,\mu \, R^2 \, v_i}{(\rho_p - \rho)\, v_i^2 \, N_e \, H} $$

$$ d_{pc}^2 = \frac{9 \,\mu \, R^2}{(\rho_p - \rho)\, v_i \, N_e \, H} $$

Using $H = D/2$ and $W = B$ (Lapple's notation), with the body-radius relation, the standard textbook form emerges:

$$ \boxed{\, d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \,\mu\, W}{2 \,\pi\, N_e \, v_i \,(\rho_p - \rho)} }\,} $$

where $W$ is the inlet width.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Single-Particle Grade Efficiency (Lapple 1951)

Define the dimensionless ratio:

$$ x = \frac{d_p}{d_{pc}} $$

The single-particle **grade efficiency** $\eta(d_p)$ is the fraction of particles of size $d_p$ that are collected. Starting from a particle-balance over a thin cylindrical shell at radius $r$ (where the particle has been drifting for time $t_g$):

$$ \eta(d_p) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\frac{2 \,(C\psi)^{-0.182}}{N_e}\cdot \frac{v_{ts} \, N_e}{v_i} \cdot \frac{A_{in}}{A_{wall}} \right] $$

After dimensional simplification (Lapple 1951), and noting that $\psi = \rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v_i / (18 \mu D)$ (the inertial parameter):

$$ \boxed{\, \eta(d_p) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-2\,(C\psi)^{-1.08}\,\left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{pc}}\right)^2\right]\,} $$

> **Key Behaviour:**
> - When $d_p \gg d_{pc}$, the exponent $\to -\infty$, so $\eta \to 1$ (complete collection).
> - When $d_p \ll d_{pc}$, $\eta \to 0$ (particle follows gas lines).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Stairmand Pressure Drop

**Stairmand (1951)** developed the most widely used pressure drop correlation by performing a force balance on the gas inside the vortex.

### Step 1 — Force on Gas Element

The radial pressure gradient within the forced vortex (Rankine vortex core) is:

$$ \frac{dP}{dr} = \frac{\rho \, v_\theta^2}{r} $$

### Step 2 — Integration Across the Vortex

Integrate from the wall ($r = R$) to the vortex core ($r = r_c$):

$$ \Delta P_{vortex} = \int_{r_c}^{R} \frac{\rho \, v_\theta^2}{r} \, dr $$

Using $v_\theta \approx v_i$ (assumed constant in the forced region):

$$ \Delta P_{vortex} = \rho \, v_i^2 \, \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_c}\right) $$

For typical high-efficiency cyclones, $\ln(R/r_c) \approx 8$, giving $\Delta P \approx 8 \rho v_i^2$.

### Step 3 — Adding Inlet & Exit Losses

Stairmand added empirical coefficients for inlet contraction, wall friction, and exit contraction, yielding the **dimensionless form**:

$$ \boxed{\, \Delta P = \xi \, \dfrac{\rho \, v_i^2}{2}\,} $$

where $\xi$ (Greek *xi*) is the **loss coefficient**:

- $\xi \approx 16$ for **high-efficiency** (Stairmand) design
- $\xi \approx 7.5$ for **Shepherd–Lapple** empirical form (used when geometry is unknown)
- $\xi \approx 8$ for **high-throughput** (Stairmand) design

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A cyclone separator has body diameter $D = 0.8$ m, inlet height $H = 0.4$ m, inlet width $B = 0.2$ m, gas flow rate $Q = 1.5$ m³/s, and effective number of turns $N_e = 5$. Gas viscosity $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, gas density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, particle density $\rho_p = 2200$ kg/m³. Determine: (i) Inlet velocity; (ii) Critical particle diameter; (iii) Pressure drop (Stairmand).

### Solution

**Step 1 — Inlet Velocity:**

$$ v_i = \frac{Q}{B \cdot H} = \frac{1.5}{0.2 \times 0.4} = \frac{1.5}{0.08} = 18.75 \;\text{m/s} $$

**Step 2 — Critical Particle Diameter** (using Lapple's formula, $W = B = 0.2$ m):

$$ d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times (1.85 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.2}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 18.75 \times (2200 - 1.2)}} $$

$$ = \sqrt{\frac{3.33 \times 10^{-5}}{1.319 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{2.524 \times 10^{-11}} $$

$$ \boxed{d_{pc} = 5.02 \,\mu m} $$

**Step 3 — Stairmand Pressure Drop** ($\xi = 16$):

$$ \Delta P = 16 \times \frac{1.2 \times (18.75)^2}{2} = 16 \times 210.9 = 3375 \;\text{Pa} $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta P \approx 3.38 \; \text{kPa}} $$

**Interpretation:** The cyclone can theoretically collect all particles larger than **5 $\mu m** with 100 % efficiency, and the operating energy cost corresponds to a pressure drop of about 3.4 kPa across the unit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> A physical cyclone has a complex 3-D helical flow structure that is best visualized with a CAD drawing. In compliance with the KTU-Premier-Engine schematic protocol, the Mermaid block below renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the cyclone's internal operating zones, including all process streams, intermediate vortex regions, and material discharge paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet Duct - Dust Laden Gas] --> B[Tangential Inlet Box - Width B Height H]
    B --> C[Outer Annular Vortex - Downward Helical Flow]
    C --> D[Cylindrical Barrel - Length L_b]
    D --> E[Conical Section - Length L_c Apex Convergence]
    E --> F[Apex Turnaround Region - Inner Vortex Inversion]
    F --> G[Dust Hopper - Gravitational Discharge]
    G --> H[Dust Collection Drum or Lock Hopper]
    C --> I[Inner Core Vortex - Upward Helical Flow]
    F --> I
    I --> J[Vortex Finder Tube - Diameter D_e]
    J --> K[Cleaned Gas Outlet Plenum]
    K --> L[ID Fan or Stack Discharge]
    M[Hopper Seal Air] --> G

    style A fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#aa0000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#d6e4ff,stroke:#0033aa,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#aa7700,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#444444,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.1 Process Stream Identification Table

| Stream Symbol | Phase | Direction | Composition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A** | Gas–solid suspension | Inlet | Dust-laden carrier gas |
| **I** | Gas only | Upward (inner core) | Partially cleaned gas |
| **K** | Gas only | Upward (outlet) | Cleaned gas (≤ spec'd emission) |
| **G** | Solid (dust) | Downward (gravity) | Collected particulate |
| **M** | Air (sealing) | Inward (purge) | Prevents leakage back into vortex |

## 4.2 Internal Vortex Topology (Textual Schematic)

```
                 ┌──── CLEAN GAS OUTLET ────┐
                 │                           │
                 │     ╱ VORTEX FINDER ╲     │
                 │    │                  │   │
                 │    │  INNER CORE      │   │
   INLET  ══════►│    │  VORTEX (UP)     │   │
   (tangential)  │    │                  │   │
                 │    ╲__________________╱   │
                 │                            │
                 │   OUTER ANNULAR VORTEX      │
                 │   (DOWN along wall)         │
                 │        ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓        │
                 │        ▓  DUST WALL  ▓      │
                 │        ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓        │
                 │         ╲___________╱       │
                 │             ▲                │
                 │           HOPPER             │
                 │             │                │
                 └─── DUST DISCHARGE ────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Vortex Concept:** A reverse-flow cyclone is unique because it hosts **two simultaneous, counter-rotating vortices** sharing a common axis — the **outer annular vortex** (downward, near the wall) and the **inner core vortex** (upward, along the axis). This double-vortex structure is the *signature feature* that gives cyclones their separation efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` 

**Define a centrifugal collector. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a cyclone separator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **centrifugal collector** is a dry-type particulate control device that uses the centrifugal force generated by a swirling gas stream to separate solid or liquid particles from the carrier gas without employing any moving parts in the gas path.

**Working (1 mark each for the 3 key points):**
1. The dust-laden gas enters **tangentially** through a rectangular inlet duct, creating a strong swirling motion inside the cylindrical body.
2. Particles, due to their greater inertia, are flung **outward against the wall** and slide down into the dust hopper, while the gas spirals down and reverses direction to escape upward.
3. The **cleaned gas** exits axially through the central *vortex finder* tube at the top.

**Sketch:** (Refer to SECTION 4.2 schematic for the figure)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Explain the concept of "critical particle diameter" in cyclone design. Write the formula for it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **critical particle diameter ($d_{pc}$)** is defined as the diameter of the smallest particle that, under the action of the centrifugal field, will *just* be collected by the cyclone with **50 % theoretical efficiency** when operating at the design inlet velocity.

**Significance (1.5 marks):** Particles larger than $d_{pc}$ are collected with high efficiency, while particles smaller than $d_{pc}$ are largely lost with the cleaned gas stream. It serves as the **primary design index** of cyclone performance.

**Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$ d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \,\mu\, W}{2 \,\pi\, N_e \, v_i \,(\rho_p - \rho)}} $$

where $W$ is the inlet width, $N_e$ the number of effective turns, $v_i$ the inlet velocity, and $\rho_p - \rho$ the density difference between particle and gas.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - In derivation questions, examiners award **2 of the 7 marks** *strictly* for the final boxed formula. **You must write the final formula inside a box** (or clearly underline it) to claim these marks.
> - In numerical problems, **always carry units** through every step. Students commonly lose 1 mark by writing a numerical answer like *"$d_{pc} = 5$"* instead of *"$d_{pc} = 5 \,\mu m$"*.
> - In cyclone sketches, examiners check for **3 essential labels**: (i) Tangential inlet, (ii) Vortex finder, (iii) Dust hopper. Missing any one costs **1 mark**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Design & Calculation Track

#### Q.A(a) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Derive an expression for the collection efficiency of a cyclone separator using Lapple's model, clearly stating the assumptions.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions of Lapple's Model (2 marks):**
1. The flow inside the cyclone approximates a **forced vortex** (constant tangential velocity).
2. Particle motion is governed by **Stokes' law** ($Re_p < 0.1$); hence drag is linearly proportional to velocity.
3. Particles are spherical and of uniform density.
4. The gas is incompressible and the inlet velocity is uniform across the inlet area.
5. Re-entrainment of collected dust is neglected.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Radial drift velocity of a particle under centrifugal–drag balance:

$$ v_r = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_\theta^2}{18 \,\mu \, r} $$

**Step 2:** The radial distance $r$ that a particle reaches after time $t$ is obtained by integrating:

$$ \int_{r_0}^{r} r \, dr = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_\theta^2}{18 \,\mu} \int_0^t dt $$

$$ r^2 = r_0^2 + \frac{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_\theta^2 \, t}{9 \,\mu} $$

**Step 3:** The particle residence time in the outer vortex is:

$$ t = \frac{N_e \, H}{v_i} $$

**Step 4:** Collection occurs if the particle reaches the wall ($r = R$). Combining and defining $d_{pc}$ as the diameter where the particle just reaches the wall starting from the centre ($r_0 = 0$):

$$ R^2 = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho)\, d_p^2 \, v_i^2}{9 \,\mu} \cdot \frac{N_e H}{v_i} \cdot \frac{1}{C\psi} $$

where $C$ is Cunningham's slip correction and $\psi$ is the inertial parameter. After algebraic reduction, we obtain the **grade efficiency**:

$$ \boxed{\, \eta(d_p) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-2\,(C\psi)^{-1.08}\,\left(\dfrac{d_p}{d_{pc}}\right)^2\right]\,} $$

---

#### Q.A(b) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

A cyclone separator is to be designed to treat **2.0 m³/s** of flue gas at **150 °C** with dust of density **1800 kg/m³**. The cyclone dimensions are: body diameter $D = 1.0$ m, inlet height $H = 0.5$ m, inlet width $B = 0.25$ m, effective turns $N_e = 5$. At 150 °C, gas viscosity $\mu = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s and gas density $\rho = 0.835$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) Inlet velocity, (ii) Critical particle diameter, (iii) Overall grade efficiency for a 10 $\mu m$ particle. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step (i): Inlet Velocity (2 marks)**

$$ v_i = \frac{Q}{B \cdot H} = \frac{2.0}{0.25 \times 0.5} = \frac{2.0}{0.125} = 16.0 \;\text{m/s} $$

**Step (ii): Critical Particle Diameter (2 marks)**

$$ d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \,\mu\, W}{2 \,\pi\, N_e \, v_i \,(\rho_p - \rho)}} $$

$$ = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times (2.4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.25}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 16.0 \times (1800 - 0.835)}} $$

$$ = \sqrt{\frac{5.4 \times 10^{-5}}{9.018 \times 10^{5}}} = \sqrt{5.99 \times 10^{-11}} $$

$$ \boxed{d_{pc} = 7.74 \,\mu m} $$

**Step (iii): Grade Efficiency (3 marks)**

Using $C \approx 1$ (for $d_p > 1 \mu m$), and the inertial parameter $\psi = \rho_p d_p^2 v_i / (18 \mu D)$:

$$ \psi = \frac{1800 \times (10 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 16.0}{18 \times (2.4 \times 10^{-5}) \times 1.0} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^{-6}}{4.32 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ (C\psi)^{-1.08} = (6.667 \times 10^{-3})^{-1.08} \approx 246.3 $$

$$ (d_p/d_{pc})^2 = (10 / 7.74)^2 = 1.669 $$

$$ \eta = 1 - \exp[-2 \times 246.3 \times 1.669] = 1 - \exp[-822.2] $$

$$ \eta \approx 1 - 0 = \boxed{1.00 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 100\%} $$

**Interpretation:** Because 10 $\mu m > d_{pc}$, the particle is essentially **fully collected**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Descriptive & Alternative Track

#### Q.B(a) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a cyclone separator. Discuss the factors affecting its collection efficiency.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A cyclone separator consists of the following components:
1. **Cylindrical body / barrel** of diameter $D$ and length $L_b$.
2. **Tangential inlet duct** of dimensions $B \times H$ (rectangular).
3. **Conical bottom section** of height $L_c$, converging to a dust outlet.
4. **Vortex finder (inner tube)** at the top, of diameter $D_e = D/2$.
5. **Dust hopper** at the bottom, fitted with a rotary valve or double-flap for dust discharge.
6. **Cleaned gas outlet plenum** connected to an induced-draft fan.

**Working (2 marks):**
Refer to the 5-stage description in **SECTION 2.1** of these notes — tangential injection, outer vortex, apex turnaround, inner vortex, particulate discharge.

**Factors Affecting Efficiency (2 marks):**
1. **Inlet velocity** — Higher $v_i$ improves efficiency up to ~25 m/s, beyond which turbulence causes re-entrainment.
2. **Body diameter** — Smaller $D$ means higher centrifugal acceleration; efficiency $\propto 1/\sqrt{D}$.
3. **Gas viscosity** — Higher $\mu$ (e.g., hot gases) reduces efficiency.
4. **Particle density & size** — Denser, larger particles are collected more easily.
5. **Solid loading** — Very high dust loadings can degrade efficiency due to particle–particle interactions.
6. **Hopper sealing** — Inadequate seal allows re-entrainment of collected dust.

---

#### Q.B(b) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Calculate the pressure drop across a cyclone separator using (i) Stairmand's equation and (ii) Shepherd–Lapple's empirical equation. Given: inlet velocity $v_i = 22$ m/s, gas density $\rho = 1.15$ kg/m³. Use $\xi = 16$ for Stairmand's high-efficiency design.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data (0.5 marks):**
$v_i = 22$ m/s, $\rho = 1.15$ kg/m³, $\xi_{\text{Stairmand}} = 16$.

**Step 1: Velocity Head (1 mark)**

$$ \frac{\rho \, v_i^2}{2} = \frac{1.15 \times (22)^2}{2} = \frac{1.15 \times 484}{2} = 278.3 \;\text{Pa} $$

**Step 2: Stairmand Pressure Drop (2.5 marks)**

$$ \Delta P_{\text{Stairmand}} = \xi \cdot \frac{\rho \, v_i^2}{2} = 16 \times 278.3 $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta P_{\text{Stairmand}} = 4453 \;\text{Pa} \approx 4.45 \;\text{kPa}} $$

**Step 3: Shepherd–Lapple Pressure Drop (2.5 marks)**

The Shepherd–Lapple empirical form uses a fixed coefficient of **7.5**:

$$ \Delta P_{\text{S-L}} = 7.5 \cdot \frac{\rho \, v_i^2}{2} = 7.5 \times 278.3 $$

$$ \boxed{\Delta P_{\text{S-L}} = 2087 \;\text{Pa} \approx 2.09 \;\text{kPa}} $$

**Step 4: Discussion (0.5 marks)**

The Stairmand estimate is higher because it conservatively accounts for **all losses** (wall friction, inlet contraction, exit contraction, and vortex dissipation), whereas Shepherd–Lapple approximates only the **net cyclone-body** losses. In practice, designers prefer the higher Stairmand value for *fan sizing* to ensure adequate blower capacity.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ A **cyclone separator** is a dry, no-moving-parts centrifugal collector using **two counter-rotating vortices**.
- ✅ The **five operating stages**: tangential entry → outer down vortex → apex reversal → inner up vortex → cleaned gas exit.
- ✅ **Inlet velocity** must lie in the range **15 – 25 m/s** for optimum performance.
- ✅ **Critical particle diameter** is the most-cited design index; smaller $d_{pc}$ ⇒ higher efficiency.
- ✅ Key parameters and their effect on efficiency:
  - Increase $v_i$ → ↓ $d_{pc}$ (better)
  - Decrease $D$ → ↑ centrifugal force → ↓ $d_{pc}$ (better)
  - Increase $\rho_p$ → ↓ $d_{pc}$ (better)
  - Increase $\mu$ → ↑ $d_{pc}$ (worse — this is why hot cyclones are less efficient)
- ✅ **Stairmand high-efficiency** proportions: $H = D/2$, $B = D/5$, $L_b = 1.5D$, $L_c = 2D$.
- ✅ **Pressure drop** typically 500 – 2500 Pa; Stairmand formula $\Delta P = \xi \rho v_i^2 / 2$ with $\xi = 16$ for high-efficiency design.
- ✅ **Shepherd–Lapple** simplified form uses $\xi = 7.5$ (use only when geometry is unspecified).
- ✅ **Multi-cyclones** (battery cyclones) use small $D$ (50 – 150 mm) to achieve lower $d_{pc}$ at high gas flow rates.
- ✅ Cyclones are **inefficient for sub-micron and sticky particles**; always pair with a downstream fabric filter or ESP for fine dust.
- ✅ Always mention **$C$ (Cunningham slip correction)** when working with particles below 1 $\mu m$.
- ✅ The **Lapple grade efficiency** formula in its boxed form is mandatory in derivations.
- ✅ In numerical problems: **carry SI units** in every line, and **box your final answer**.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Centrifugal Collectors**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wet Collectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wet Collectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wet Collector** (also referred to as a **Wet Scrubber** or **Gas Scrubbing Device**) is a unit operation equipment used in process industries to remove particulate matter, mists, and/or gaseous pollutants from a contaminated gas stream by bringing it into intimate contact with a liquid (typically water, or an aqueous chemical solution). The transfer mechanism is governed by the combined principles of **inertial impaction, diffusion (Brownian), interception, and condensation**, while mass transfer between phases follows the two-film theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Wet collectors are air pollution control devices that employ a liquid medium to capture contaminants from gas streams through mechanisms of momentum transfer, diffusion, and absorption. They are uniquely capable of simultaneously removing particulates and gaseous pollutants.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Think of it like washing dust off your car with a garden hose.** When you spray water on a dusty car, the water droplets "stick" to dust particles, and the dirty water droplets run off, taking the dust with them. A wet collector works on the *exact same principle* at an industrial scale.

- **The dusty car** = The polluted hot gas stream (flue gas from an incinerator or process reactor)
- **The garden hose spray** = The scrubbing liquid (water/chemical reagent) sprayed as fine droplets
- **The dirty water runoff** = The sludge/wash liquor collected at the bottom
- **The car surface** = The contact zone inside the scrubber where gas-liquid interaction happens

The *finer* the water spray, the *better* it scrubs dust — just like finer mist cleans a windshield more effectively than a thick stream of water. The *faster* the gas and the *closer* the contact, the *more efficient* the dust removal.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification Adopted by KTU Examiners**

Wet collectors are broadly classified based on **energy input method** and **gas-liquid contacting principle**:

| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| **Low-Energy / Gravity Type** | Spray Towers, Sieve-Plate Towers, Packed Bed Scrubbers |
| **Medium-Energy / Impingement Type** | Cyclonic Scrubbers, Impingement Plates |
| **High-Energy / Atomization Type** | Venturi Scrubbers, Orifice Scrubbers |
| **Mechanically Aided** | Rotating Fan Scrubbers, Disintegrator Scrubbers |
| **Wet Electrostatic** | Wet ESP (Combination Device) |

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Collection Efficiency (η)** – usually expressed as a percentage, e.g., **99.5%**.
- **Cut Diameter (d₅₀)** – the particle diameter collected with **50% efficiency**.
- **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** – measured in **mm H₂O** or **Pa** or **in. w.g.**
- **Liquid-to-Gas Ratio (L/G)** – typically in **L/m³**.
- **Contacting Power (PT)** – energy required per unit volume of gas treated, in **kJ/m³** or **hp per 1000 ft³/min**.
- **Gas Absorption Factor** – dimensionless number for gas-phase pollutants.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Collection Mechanisms

A wet collector removes particles by leveraging the **inertia** of the particle versus the **drag** of the surrounding gas flow lines bending around a liquid droplet. Four primary mechanisms are active:

**Step 1 — Inertial Impaction (Dominant for dₚ > 1 μm)**
- A liquid droplet acts as a target.
- Gas streamlines bend around the droplet, but the particle — being denser — continues in a straight line due to inertia.
- If the particle's path crosses the droplet, it is captured.
- Quantified by the **Stokes Number (Stk)**.

**Step 2 — Diffusion (Dominant for dₚ < 0.1 μm)**
- Sub-micron particles undergo **Brownian motion**, randomly zigzagging.
- Random walk brings them into contact with droplets.
- Quantified by the **Peclet Number (Pe)**.

**Step 3 — Direct Interception (Important for 0.1–1 μm)**
- The particle grazes the droplet surface without inertial deviation.
- Occurs when the particle's centerline passes within one particle radius of the droplet.

**Step 4 — Condensation (Enhances other mechanisms)**
- If the gas is cooled below its dew point, particles act as condensation nuclei.
- Grown particles are easier to capture.

### 2.2 Why Wet Collectors? — Real-World Utility

- Can handle **high-temperature gases** (up to 1000 °C with appropriate construction).
- Can remove **gaseous and particulate** pollutants simultaneously.
- Effective on **sticky, corrosive, or flammable dusts** (e.g., sulfuric acid mist, tar, coke dust).
- Used in **sulfuric acid plants, fertilizer plants, foundries, coke ovens, and HCl gas absorption**.

### 2.3 The Concept of "Contacting Power" (Semrau's Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Contacting Power Theory is heavily tested in KTU exams (Module 3, Part A & Part B).**

The overall collection efficiency of a wet scrubber correlates empirically with the **total power dissipated per unit volume of gas treated** (called **Contacting Power, P_T**).

$$P_T = -\frac{\Delta P}{Q_g} + P_L$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = gas-side pressure drop (Pa)
- $Q_g$ = volumetric gas flow rate (m³/s)
- $P_L$ = liquid pumping power per unit gas volume (W/m³)
- $P_T$ = total contacting power (W/m³)

Semrau's empirical correlation for a **particulate scrubber**:

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-A \cdot P_T^{B}\right)$$

Where A and B are empirical constants determined experimentally (typical values: A ≈ 1.0–10, B ≈ 0.4–1.0).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these equations for ESE and Internal Assessments.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{in} - N_{out}}{N_{in}} \times 100$ | $N$ = number/mass of particles | % |
| 2 | $\eta = 1 - e^{-N_t}$ | $N_t$ = number of transfer units (dimensionless) | — |
| 3 | $v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^{2}\left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right)}{18 \mu_g}$ (Stokes) | Terminal settling velocity of droplet | m/s |
| 4 | $Stk = \dfrac{C \cdot \rho_p \cdot d_p^{2} \cdot v}{9 \cdot \mu_g \cdot d_d}$ | Stokes Number (inertial impaction) | — |
| 5 | $P_e = \dfrac{d_d \cdot v}{D_B}$ | Peclet Number (diffusion capture) | — |
| 6 | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-A \cdot P_T^{B}\right)$ | Semrau's contact-power correlation | — |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{Venturi} = \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot v_g^{2}}{2}\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)$ | Venturi pressure drop (simplified) | Pa |
| 8 | $d_{50} = \left(\dfrac{9 \mu_g d_d}{C \rho_p v}\right)^{1/2}$ | Cut diameter for impaction | m |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{spray} \approx 0.4 \rho_g v_g^{2}$ | Spray tower pressure drop approximation | Pa |
| 10 | $E = \eta \cdot E_{single\,drop}$ | Overall efficiency (Deutsch–Anderson analogue) | — |

Where:
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $d_d$ = droplet diameter (m)
- $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho_g$ = gas density (kg/m³)
- $\mu_g$ = gas viscosity (Pa·s)
- $v$ = relative velocity (m/s)
- $D_B$ = Brownian diffusivity (m²/s)
- $C$ = Cunningham slip correction factor (dimensionless, ≈ 1 for dₚ > 1 μm)

### 2.5 Cut Diameter (d₅₀) — The Design Driver

The **d₅₀ (cut diameter)** is the particle size for which the collection efficiency is exactly 50%. KTU problems frequently ask: *"If d₅₀ is X, find efficiency at diameter Y."*

A widely used log-linear relation (for inertial scrubbers):

$$\eta(d_p) = \dfrac{\left(d_p / d_{50}\right)^{2}}{1 + \left(d_p / d_{50}\right)^{2}} \times 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **logistic-type curve** showing high efficiency for large particles and low efficiency for sub-micron particles unless supplemented by diffusion or condensation mechanisms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)

This is the **starting point** for understanding droplet trajectory and capture in any wet scrubber.

**Statement:** A spherical liquid droplet falling under gravity in a gas stream reaches a constant velocity when the **gravitational force** equals the **drag force**.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a falling droplet (steady state)**

The droplet is acted upon by:
- **Gravitational force (downward):** $F_g = m_p \cdot g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_d^{3} \rho_p \cdot g$
- **Buoyancy force (upward):** $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_d^{3} \rho_g \cdot g$
- **Drag force (opposing motion):** $F_d = C_D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g v_t^{2}}{2} = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_d^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g v_t^{2}}{2}$

**Step 2 — Set net force to zero at terminal velocity**

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_d^{3}\left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right) g = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_d^{2}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_g v_t^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel common terms**

$$\dfrac{1}{6} d_d \left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right) g = C_D \cdot \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_g v_t^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the laminar-flow drag coefficient (Stokes' regime, Re < 0.1)**

For Stokes' flow: $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re} = \dfrac{24 \mu_g}{\rho_g v_t d_d}$

**Step 5 — Substitute $C_D$ and simplify**

$$\dfrac{1}{6} d_d \left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right) g = \dfrac{24 \mu_g}{\rho_g v_t d_d} \cdot \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_g v_t^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{6} d_d \left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right) g = 3 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_g v_t}{d_d}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $v_t$**

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_d^{2} \left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right)}{18 \mu_g}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_d^{2}\left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right)}{18 \mu_g}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows that **droplet settling velocity scales with the SQUARE of droplet diameter** — halving the droplet diameter reduces settling velocity by 4×, which is why fine sprays give better contact time.

### 3.2 Derivation: Collection Efficiency for Inertial Impaction (Single-Drop)

**Step 1 — Define the Single-Drop Collection Efficiency (η_d)**

$$\eta_d = \dfrac{\text{Particles collected by one drop}}{\text{Particles flowing through drop's cross-section}}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric argument (Langmuir–Blodgett type)**

Consider a droplet of diameter $d_d$ sweeping a cylindrical gas column of radius $r_c$ as it falls. The particle is captured if its trajectory brings it within distance $d_p/2$ of the droplet.

$$r_c = \dfrac{d_d}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\eta_d}$$

For a particle that would miss the drop, the centripetal distance equals the impact parameter.

**Step 3 — Impaction Parameter (K) Definition**

$$K = \dfrac{\tau \cdot v}{d_d} = \dfrac{C \rho_p d_p^{2} v}{18 \mu_g d_d} = \dfrac{Stk \cdot 2}{1}$$

The single-drop efficiency is empirically related to K (Langmuir):

$$\eta_d = \dfrac{K^{2}}{(K + 0.25)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Final expression**

$$\boxed{\,\eta_d = \dfrac{\left[\dfrac{C \rho_p d_p^{2} v}{18 \mu_g d_d}\right]^{2}}{\left[\dfrac{C \rho_p d_p^{2} v}{18 \mu_g d_d} + 0.25\right]^{2}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows the *non-linear* dependence of efficiency on particle size — efficiency is low for very small particles (low K) and high for large particles (high K), explaining why scrubbers struggle with sub-micron dust.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Venturi Scrubber Cut Diameter

**Given:** A venturi scrubber operates with throat gas velocity $v_g = 80$ m/s, throat diameter $D_t = 0.4$ m, droplet diameter $d_d = 100 \mu m$, gas viscosity $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, particle density $\rho_p = 2000$ kg/m³, slip factor $C = 1.1$.

**Find:** Cut diameter $d_{50}$.

**Step 1 — Use the impaction-based d₅₀ formula**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu_g d_d}{C \rho_p v_g}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) \times (100 \times 10^{-6})}{1.1 \times 2000 \times 80}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation**

$$9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-9} = 1.62 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation**

$$1.1 \times 2000 \times 80 = 1.76 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 5 — Division**

$$\dfrac{1.62 \times 10^{-8}}{1.76 \times 10^{5}} = 9.20 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Step 6 — Square root**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{9.20 \times 10^{-14}} \approx 3.03 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{50} \approx 0.30 \; \mu\text{m}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The scrubber achieves 50% efficiency only on particles as small as 0.30 μm. For sub-micron particulate control, **venturi scrubbers are still the most efficient choice** in wet collection.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Contacting Power (Semrau) Efficiency

**Given:** A spray tower has gas pressure drop $\Delta P = 250$ Pa, gas flow rate $Q_g = 5$ m³/s, liquid pumping power $P_L = 80$ W, and empirical constants $A = 2.5$, $B = 0.7$.

**Step 1 — Compute Contacting Power $P_T$**

$$P_T = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q_g} + P_L = \dfrac{250}{5} + 80 = 50 + 80 = 130 \text{ W/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Semrau equation**

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-A \cdot P_T^{B}\right) = 1 - \exp\left(-2.5 \times 130^{0.7}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $130^{0.7}$**

$$130^{0.7} = e^{0.7 \ln 130} = e^{0.7 \times 4.868} = e^{3.408} \approx 30.18$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-2.5 \times 30.18} = 1 - e^{-75.45} \approx 1 - 2.4 \times 10^{-33} \approx 1.0$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta \approx 99.99\%\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This illustrates the **empirical power of contacting theory** — high contacting power saturates the efficiency at near 100%. The practical design challenge is balancing $P_T$ against energy cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — General Wet Scrubber System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Polluted Gas Inlet] --> B1[Inlet Duct with Turning Vanes]
    B1 --> C1[Pre-Cooling or Quench Section]
    C1 --> D1[Gas-Liquid Contacting Zone]
    D2[Liquid Storage Tank] --> E1[Liquid Pump]
    E1 --> F1[Atomizer or Spray Nozzle Bank]
    F1 --> D1
    D1 --> G1[Entrainment Eliminator Demister Pad]
    G1 --> H1[Clean Gas Stack Outlet]
    D1 --> I1[Sludge Collection Hopper]
    I1 --> J1[Sludge Treatment and Recycle Loop]
    J1 --> D2
    style A1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000
    style H1 fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600
    style D1 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    style I1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The polluted gas enters from the upper left.
- It is **pre-conditioned** (cooling/quenching) to reduce volume and protect downstream equipment.
- In the central **Contacting Zone**, the gas meets atomized liquid droplets.
- The **cleaned gas** passes through a **demister pad** to strip entrained liquid droplets.
- The **dirty liquid** falls into a **sludge hopper**, which is recycled or treated.

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture of Three Major Scrubber Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Spray Tower] --> A2[Low Energy 250 to 1000 Pa]
    B1[Venturi Scrubber] --> B2[High Energy 2500 to 20000 Pa]
    C1[Impingement Plate Scrubber] --> C2[Medium Energy 500 to 2500 Pa]
    A2 --> D1[Collection Efficiency 60 to 80 percent for 5 um]
    B2 --> D2[Collection Efficiency 99 plus for sub micron]
    C2 --> D3[Collection Efficiency 85 to 95 percent for 2 to 5 um]
    style A1 fill:#fff0b3
    style B1 fill:#ffb3b3
    style C1 fill:#b3d9ff
    style D2 fill:#c6efce
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Wet Collector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Pollutant Type and Size Selection] --> Q1{Is pollutant a gas or particle}
    Q1 -- Gas --> Q2{Absorption needed}
    Q1 -- Particle --> Q3{Particle size range}
    Q2 -- Yes Acidic or Alkaline --> R1[Packed Bed Scrubber]
    Q2 -- Soluble in water --> R2[Spray Tower]
    Q3 -- Larger than 10 um --> R3[Low Energy Spray Tower]
    Q3 -- 1 to 10 um --> R4[Impingement or Cyclonic]
    Q3 -- Smaller than 1 um --> R5[High Energy Venturi Scrubber]
    R1 --> O1[Final Selection: Packed Scrubber]
    R2 --> O1
    R3 --> O1
    R4 --> O1
    R5 --> O1
    style S1 fill:#cce6ff
    style O1 fill:#c6efce,stroke:#006600
    style Q1 fill:#ffe6cc
    style Q2 fill:#ffe6cc
    style Q3 fill:#ffe6cc
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Venturi Scrubber (Most Tested in KTU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Dirty Hot Gas] --> V1[Converging Section]
    V1 --> V2[Throat High Velocity Zone]
    L1[Liquid from Pump] --> N1[Atomizing Nozzle in Throat]
    N1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3[Diverging Section]
    V3 --> S1[Scrubbing Reaction Zone]
    S1 --> M1[Demister Mist Eliminator]
    M1 --> P2[Clean Saturated Gas to Stack]
    S1 --> SL1[Sludge to Treatment]
    style V2 fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000
    style N1 fill:#cce6ff
    style M1 fill:#fff0b3
    style SL1 fill:#e0e0e0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the Venturi diagram in an exam:** The **throat** is the heart of the device. Gas accelerates to 50–200 m/s, creating a **low-pressure zone** that atomizes the injected liquid into fine droplets (50–500 μm). The high-velocity droplets capture particles via **inertial impaction**; the gas then decelerates in the diverging section, recovering pressure and promoting droplet coagulation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> *Define a wet scrubber. Mention any two advantages and one limitation of wet collectors compared to dry ESPs.*

**Model Answer:**

A wet scrubber is an air pollution control device that uses a liquid (usually water) to remove particulate and/or gaseous pollutants from a gas stream by intimate gas-liquid contact. The removal mechanisms include inertial impaction, diffusion, interception, and absorption.

**Advantages:**
1. Can simultaneously remove particulates and gases (e.g., SO₂ and dust).
2. Can handle hot, corrosive, sticky, or explosive dusts safely.
3. No fire/explosion hazard in handling flammable dusts.

**Limitation:**
1. Generates a wet sludge that requires further treatment and disposal.
2. Corrosion of internal components due to acidic or alkaline scrubbing liquor.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Series A]**
> *What is meant by 'Contacting Power' in wet scrubber design? State Semrau's equation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Contacting Power (P_T):** It is the total power (gas-phase pressure drop energy + liquid pumping energy) dissipated per unit volume of gas treated, expressed in W/m³ or hp/(1000 ft³/min). It is the empirical design parameter that correlates with overall collection efficiency.

$$P_T = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q_g} + P_L$$

**Semrau's Equation:**

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-A \cdot P_T^{B}\right)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants depending on the type of scrubber and dust characteristics.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / June 2024 - ESE Module 3 Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a **Venturi Scrubber**. State its main applications. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A venturi scrubber treats 10 m³/s of flue gas with a throat gas velocity of 90 m/s. The L/G ratio is 1.2 L/m³, and the throat diameter is 0.35 m. Calculate:
- (i) The pressure drop across the venturi.
- (ii) The cut diameter $d_{50}$ if droplet diameter is 80 μm, gas viscosity is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, and particle density is 1800 kg/m³. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Construction and Working of Venturi Scrubber:**

A venturi scrubber consists of three sections:

**1. Converging Section:**
- Funnel-shaped entry that accelerates the dirty gas from duct velocity (≈ 15 m/s) to throat velocity (50–200 m/s).
- Angle of convergence typically 25°–30°.

**2. Throat:**
- A constricted cylindrical section of length 0.5–1 m where the gas reaches its **maximum velocity**.
- A **liquid injection nozzle** is fitted at or just upstream of the throat.
- The high-velocity gas shears the injected liquid into a fine **spray of 50–500 μm droplets**.

**3. Diverging Section:**
- A diverging cone (≈ 7° expansion angle) that decelerates the gas.
- Recovers about 70–80% of the kinetic energy as **static pressure**.

**Working Principle:**
- In the throat, droplets and dust particles meet under extreme turbulence.
- **Inertial impaction** dominates: the dense particles cannot follow the gas streamlines around droplets and are captured.
- The droplet + dust agglomerate is carried into the diverging section, where the **velocity drop** promotes coagulation and settling.
- A downstream **cyclone separator or mist eliminator** removes the wet slurry.

**Applications:**
- Control of sub-micron particulates from metallurgical furnaces.
- SO₂ absorption with alkaline slurry.
- Coke oven gas cleaning.
- Tar and oil mist removal.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_g = 10$ m³/s
- $v_g = 90$ m/s (throat)
- $L/G = 1.2$ L/m³ $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/m³
- $D_t = 0.35$ m
- $d_d = 80 \mu m = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³
- Assume air: $\rho_g \approx 1.2$ kg/m³, $C \approx 1$ (Cunningham factor)

**(i) Pressure Drop:**

Using the simplified venturi equation:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho_g v_g^{2}}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right) \times 10^{3}$$

(Where the factor 10³ converts L/m³ to dimensionless ratio for the pressure-drop multiplier.)

Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{1.2 \times 90^{2}}{2} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.2 \times 8100}{2} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 4860 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 5.832 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P \approx 5.83 \text{ Pa}\,}$$

> **Note:** The simplified equation gives the *contribution* of L/G. The total venturi pressure drop (more rigorous form) is:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \dfrac{\rho_g v_g^{2}}{2}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{D_t}{D_u}\right)^{4}\right] + \rho_L g h_L$$

where $D_u$ is the upstream duct diameter. For a typical duct of 0.7 m, the upstream-area pressure-recovery component dominates and the *net* $\Delta P$ is in the range **1500–3500 Pa** for industrial venturi scrubbers.

**(ii) Cut Diameter:**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu_g d_d}{C \rho_p v_g}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 1800 \times 90}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$9 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} = 9 \times 1.8 \times 80 \times 10^{-11} = 1296 \times 10^{-11} = 1.296 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Denominator:**

$$1800 \times 90 = 162000 = 1.62 \times 10^{5}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{1.296 \times 10^{-8}}{1.62 \times 10^{5}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Square root:**

$$d_{50} = \sqrt{8.0 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.83 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{50} \approx 0.283 \; \mu\text{m}\,}$$

**Conclusion:** The venturi scrubber in this design achieves 50% collection of particles as small as 0.28 μm — confirming it as a high-efficiency device suitable for **sub-micron particulate control**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - ESE Module 3 Set A]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **Packed Bed Scrubber** and **Cyclonic Scrubber** with suitable diagrams. Compare their pressure drops and typical efficiency ranges. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Stokes' law terminal settling velocity** for a liquid droplet in a wet scrubber. An effluent gas stream contains 5-μm particles of density 1200 kg/m³. The scrubber uses water droplets of 200 μm diameter. If the gas viscosity is $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, calculate the **terminal settling velocity of the droplet** and comment on the droplet's residence time. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Packed vs. Cyclonic Scrubber — Comparison:**

| Feature | Packed Bed Scrubber | Cyclonic Scrubber |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Energy input | Low–medium (gas pressure drop) | Medium (centrifugal) |
| Pressure drop | 500–2500 Pa | 750–2000 Pa |
| Best for | Gas absorption + fine particles | Coarse to medium particles (>5 μm) |
| Liquid distribution | Critical (maldistribution reduces efficiency) | Centrifugal action of gas |
| Plugging tendency | High (with sticky dust) | Low |
| Typical efficiency | 90–98% (with chemical reaction) | 80–95% |

**Packed Bed Working:** Gas flows upward (or downward) through a packed bed of **Raschig rings, Pall rings, or Berl saddles** while scrubbing liquid is sprayed from the top. The packing provides a large wetted surface area for gas-liquid mass transfer. Used for SO₂, HCl, NH₃ absorption.

**Cyclonic Scrubber Working:** The gas enters tangentially, creating a **vortex** that slings water droplets (and particles) against the wall by centrifugal force. The slurry flows down the wall and is collected at the bottom. Used for sticky dusts in metallurgical industries.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical:**

*Derivation is shown in Section 3.1 (same equation).*

**Given:**
- $\rho_p = 1200$ kg/m³ (note: for a liquid droplet, use $\rho_d$ = 1000 kg/m³)
- $d_d = 200 \mu m = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\rho_g \approx 1.2$ kg/m³ (air)

**Stokes' Terminal Velocity:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{g \cdot d_d^{2} \left(\rho_d - \rho_g\right)}{18 \mu_g}$$

$$= \dfrac{9.81 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (1000 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 998.8 = 9.81 \times 3.9952 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.92 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Denominator:**

$$18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 3.24 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Result:**

$$v_t = \dfrac{3.92 \times 10^{-4}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.21 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_t \approx 1.21 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

**Residence Time Comment:**

If the scrubber has an effective contacting height of 3 m, the droplet residence time is:

$$\tau = \dfrac{h}{v_t} = \dfrac{3}{1.21} \approx 2.48 \text{ s}$$

This residence time is **sufficient for efficient particulate capture** in counter-current or co-current operation. Smaller droplets settle slower, providing longer residence time but worse impact capture (low K); larger droplets settle faster but have smaller surface area. The **optimal droplet size is typically 100–500 μm** for industrial wet scrubbers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert micrometers (μm) to meters (m) in $d_d$. This is the single most common error in Part B numericals. Loss: **3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using particle density in droplet settling velocity. Remember — $v_t$ uses **droplet** density vs. gas density, not the dust density. Loss: **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing Semrau's equation without stating that $A$ and $B$ are *empirical constants*. Examiners expect the caveat. Loss: **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In sketch-based questions, drawing a venturi without labeling the **throat** and the **liquid injection point** — both are mandatory for full marks. Loss: **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Stating "wet scrubber removes dust" without specifying the **mechanism** (impaction / diffusion / absorption). Loss: **1 mark**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** A wet collector is a device that uses liquid to remove **particles and gases** from a gas stream.
- **Four Capture Mechanisms:** **Impaction** (dₚ > 1 μm), **Diffusion** (dₚ < 0.1 μm), **Interception**, **Condensation**.
- **Classification:** Low-energy (spray, packed), Medium-energy (cyclonic, impingement), High-energy (venturi).
- **Key Metrics:** $\eta$ (efficiency), $d_{50}$ (cut diameter), $\Delta P$ (pressure drop), $L/G$ (liquid-to-gas ratio), $P_T$ (contacting power).
- **Stokes' Law:** $v_t = \dfrac{g d_d^{2}(\rho_d - \rho_g)}{18 \mu_g}$ — settling velocity depends on the **square** of droplet diameter.
- **Semrau's Equation:** $\eta = 1 - \exp(-A \cdot P_T^{B})$ — links efficiency to contacting power.
- **Cut Diameter Formula:** $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu_g d_d}{C \rho_p v_g}}$ — smaller $d_{50}$ means **higher efficiency**.
- **Venturi Scrubber:** Highest efficiency (99%+ on sub-micron), highest pressure drop, used in metallurgical and sulfuric acid plants.
- **Spray Tower:** Lowest pressure drop, low efficiency on fine particles, used for gas absorption (e.g., SO₂, HCl).
- **Packed Bed:** Best for **gas absorption** with chemical reaction, susceptible to plugging.
- **Cyclonic Scrubber:** Uses centrifugal force; good for sticky/coarse particles.
- **Mole Concept Hint:** For **gas absorption** wet scrubbers, use **Henry's Law** and **Two-Film Theory**; for **particulate**, use **impaction-diffusion theories**.
- **Units Vigilance:** $\mu g$ → $m$, $Pa \cdot s$ for viscosity, $kg/m^3$ for density, $m/s$ for velocities.
- **Energy Hierarchy:** Venturi > Cyclonic > Packed > Spray (in both $\Delta P$ and typical efficiency).
- **Practical Limitation:** Generates **sludge** that requires treatment (a major operating cost).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fabric Filters and Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT613 - POLLUTION CONTROL IN PROCESS INDUSTRIES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fabric Filters and Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fabric Filters (Baghouses) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** A Fabric Filter, commonly known as a **Baghouse**, is a dry, high-efficiency particulate air pollution control device that separates dust particles from a gas stream by forcing the gas through a porous fabric filter medium. The particulates are retained on the fabric while the cleaned gas (flue gas) passes through. They are characterized by extremely high collection efficiencies (**>99.9%** for submicron particles) and are widely used in industries such as cement, steel, power generation, and pharmaceutical manufacturing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Definition to Memorize:** A fabric filter is a "dry collection device that uses a flexible, porous fabric as the filtering medium to remove solid particulates from a dust-laden gas stream by mechanisms of interception, impaction, diffusion, and electrostatic attraction." **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine you are using an extremely fine, industrial-grade **tea strainer (the bag)** to filter out tea leaves from boiling water (the dust-laden gas). The water passes through easily, but the leaves (dust) get trapped in the mesh. Over time, leaves accumulate and block the strainer. So, you tap the strainer (the cleaning mechanism) to dislodge the leaves. A fabric filter works exactly like this — except the "tea" is toxic industrial flue gas, the "leaves" are harmful PM2.5 particles, and the "tapping" is a high-pressure air pulse (pulse-jet cleaning). --- ## 1.2 Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** is a particulate control device that uses electrical forces to separate suspended dust particles from a gas stream. Particles are first electrically charged by a high-voltage **corona discharge** and then driven toward oppositely charged collecting electrodes by the resulting electric field, where they are captured and periodically removed by rapping or washing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Definition to Memorize:** An ESP is a "dry or wet device that removes suspended particulate matter from a gas stream by giving the particles an electrical charge via corona discharge and collecting them on oppositely charged electrodes under the influence of an electric field." **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English):** Think of a **magnet attracting iron filings** from a stream of air. Now replace the magnet with an **electrically charged plate** and the iron filings with **dust particles**. The dust particles are first "energized" (charged) like little magnets, then they get pulled out of the air and stick to the plate. The plate is then "rapped" (tapped firmly), and the collected dust falls into a hopper — just like cleaning iron filings off a magnet. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Performance Metric (KTU):** ESP efficiency typically ranges from **95% to 99.9%**, while fabric filters can exceed **99.99%**. Both are considered "high-efficiency" devices per the **US EPA AP-42** standards used in KTU case studies. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fabric Filters — Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Particulate Collection Mechanisms (5 Mechanisms) A fabric filter is not a simple mesh sieve. Particles are captured by **five simultaneous mechanisms**: 1. **Straining** — Particles physically larger than the fabric pores are trapped on the surface. 2. **Inertial Impaction** — High-inertia particles (large/dense) deviate from the gas streamlines and strike the fibers. 3. **Interception** — Medium-sized particles follow streamlines but touch fibers as they pass close. 4. **Diffusion (Brownian Motion)** — Submicron particles move randomly due to gas molecule collisions and collide with fibers. 5. **Electrostatic Attraction** — Naturally charged particles (or fabric) attract oppositely charged particles. ### 2.1.2 Operational Steps (Why and How) - **Step 1: Filtration Phase** — Dust-laden gas enters the baghouse and is distributed across the filter bags. Particles form a **dust cake (filter cake)**, which actually *improves* filtration efficiency over time. - **Step 2: Cake Build-up** — Pressure drop (ΔP) increases as cake thickens. A critical ΔP is reached. - **Step 3: Cleaning Phase** — The cleaning mechanism (shaker, reverse-air, or pulse-jet) is triggered. Dust falls into the **hopper** below. - **Step 4: Re-filtration** — The residual cake provides a "precoat" for the next filtration cycle, enabling high steady-state efficiency. ### 2.1.3 Types of Fabric Filters (Classification per KTU Syllabus) | Type | Cleaning Method | Application | Pressure Drop | |---|---|---|---| | **Shaker Baghouse** | Mechanical shaking | Low-volume, light dust (e.g., grain elevators) | 4–8 cm H₂O | | **Reverse-Air Baghouse** | Reversed airflow dislodges cake | Heavy dust, high temperature (e.g., cement kilns) | 4–6 cm H₂O | | **Pulse-Jet Baghouse** | Compressed air pulse | Continuous operation, finest dust (e.g., pharmaceuticals) | 4–8 cm H₂O | ### 2.1.4 Design Parameters (Critical KTU Numericals) - **Air-to-Cloth Ratio (A/C or Vf)** — Ratio of actual gas flow rate to total filter fabric area. Units: **m³/min per m²** or **cm/s (filtration velocity)**. Typical values: **0.5 – 2.0 m/min** for reverse-air, **1.0 – 3.0 m/min** for pulse-jet. - **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** — Driving force for filtration. Expressed using the equation below. - **Cake Resistance (Kc)** — Resistance offered by accumulated dust cake. - **Filter Media Resistance (Km)** — Resistance of clean fabric. --- ## 2.2 Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) — Theoretical Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Working Principle (4 Sequential Stages) 1. **Corona Discharge Generation** — A high negative DC voltage (**30–100 kV**) is applied between a **discharge electrode (wire)** and a **collecting electrode (plate)**. This ionizes surrounding gas, producing free electrons and gas ions. 2. **Particle Charging** — Dust particles passing through the corona region collide with gas ions and acquire a **negative charge** (this is the dominant charging mechanism: *bombardment charging*). 3. **Particle Migration** — Charged particles are attracted toward the positively charged collection plate under the influence of the **electric field E**. The drift speed is called the **migration velocity (w)**. 4. **Collection & Rapping** — Particles deposit on the collection plates as a dust layer. Periodic **rapping** dislodges the dust into the hopper. The cleaned gas exits via the outlet. ### 2.2.2 Types of ESP (KTU Classification) | Type | Geometry | Application | Form | |---|---|---|---| | **Plate-Wire ESP** | Vertical plates + wires | Coal-fired power plants | Dry | | **Pipe-Type ESP** | Concentric cylinders | Small flow rates, sticky dust | Dry | | **Wet ESP** | Sprayed collection surface | Acid mist, submicron aerosols | Wet | | **Two-Stage ESP** | Separate charging + collection | Air conditioning, clean rooms | Dry | ### 2.2.3 Critical Design Parameters - **Migration Velocity (w)** — Speed of charged particle toward collection electrode. Typical: **0.05 – 0.30 m/s**. - **Specific Collection Area (SCA = A/Q)** — Plate area per unit gas flow. Typical: **30 – 90 s/m** (or 300–900 ft² per 1000 acfm). - **Particle Resistivity (ρ)** — Resistance of dust layer to electrical conduction. Optimum: **10⁴ – 10¹⁰ Ω·cm**. - **Gas Velocity (v)** — Linear flow through ESP. Typical: **0.6 – 1.5 m/s**. - **Rapping Frequency** — Typically once per **5–15 minutes** per field. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) ### **Fabric Filter Equations** | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---| | $\Delta P = K_{c} \cdot v_{f} \cdot W + K_{m} \cdot v_{f}$ | Total Pressure Drop (Cake + Media) | Pa or cm H₂O | | $\dfrac{A}{C} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{c}}$ | Air-to-Cloth Ratio (inverse of filtration velocity) | m/min | | $v_{f} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{c}}$ | Filtration / Face Velocity | m/s or m/min | | $N = \dfrac{Q \cdot t_{c}}{V_{b} \cdot \Delta P_{max}}$ | Number of bags (design equation) | Dimensionless | | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{op}$ | Overall efficiency (mechanical × operational) | % | | $\Delta P = K_{2} \cdot v_{f}^{n}$ | Empirical log-log pressure drop (n ≈ 1 for viscous flow) | Pa | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The most frequently asked equation is the **pressure drop equation** and the **air-to-cloth ratio**. Always show unit conversions explicitly — Kerala university examiners deduct marks for missing units. ### **Electrostatic Precipitator Equations** | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---| | | $\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$ | **Deutsch-Anderson Equation** (Core ESP Efficiency) | Fractional (0–1) | | $SCA = \dfrac{A}{Q} = \dfrac{L}{v}$ | Specific Collection Area | s/m | | $\eta = 1 - \exp(-w \cdot SCA)$ | Alternative form of Deutsch-Anderson | Fractional | | $F = q \cdot E$ | Electric force on charged particle | N | | $w = \dfrac{q \cdot E \cdot C_{c}}{3 \cdot \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d_{p}}$ | Migration velocity (Stokes' law) | m/s | | $\dfrac{N_{p}}{N_{p}^{0}} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot L}{v \cdot d_{b}}\right)$ | Pipe-type ESP efficiency | Fractional | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-Symbol Anti-Break Rule:** Notice we use $\vert$ implicitly avoided here. In your KTU answer sheets (and here in tables), never use the bare `|` character. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to ensure markdown tables render correctly. ### **Variable Definitions** - $w$ = Migration velocity (m/s) - $A$ = Total collection plate area (m²) - $Q$ = Gas volumetric flow rate (m³/s) - $v$ = Gas velocity through ESP (m/s) - $L$ = Length of collection plate (m) - $d_b$ = Distance between plates (m) - $C_c$ = Cunningham slip correction factor (dimensionless, ~1.0–1.5) - $E$ = Electric field strength (V/m) - $q$ = Particle charge (C) - $d_p$ = Particle diameter (m) - $\mu$ = Gas viscosity (Pa·s) ### **Real-World Utility (Engineering Field Context)** - **Fabric Filters** are the "gold standard" for **toxic particulate removal** (lead, cadmium, dioxins) in **secondary lead smelters** and **medical waste incinerators**. - **ESPs** are the workhorse of **thermal power plants** in India (NTPC stations), handling millions of m³/hr of flue gas with >99.8% efficiency. - **Combined Systems:** Modern cement plants use **ESP + Fabric Filter** in series for ultra-low emission compliance with **PCB/CPCB norms** (PM < 30 mg/Nm³). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Fabric Filter Pressure Drop Equation **Starting Principle:** The pressure drop across a fabric filter is the sum of two resistances: (1) the clean filter medium resistance, and (2) the accumulated dust cake resistance. Both are proportional to the gas velocity. **Step 1: Identify the two resistance components** The filter system behaves like a **flow through a packed bed**. By Darcy's Law applied to porous media, the total pressure drop is: $$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{medium} + \Delta P_{cake}$$ **Step 2: Express the medium resistance term** The clean filter fabric has a resistance per unit velocity, defined as the **fabric drag coefficient $K_m$**: $$\Delta P_{medium} = K_{m} \cdot v_{f}$$ where $v_f$ is the filtration (face) velocity and $K_m$ is the resistance of the clean fabric (units: Pa·s/m). **Step 3: Express the cake resistance term** The dust cake also offers a resistance proportional to its mass per unit area, $W$ (kg/m²), and to velocity: $$\Delta P_{cake} = K_{c} \cdot v_{f} \cdot W$$ where $K_c$ is the **specific cake resistance** (m/kg) — a property of the dust type. **Step 4: Combine the two terms** Summing the contributions gives the **total pressure drop equation** (highly important for KTU numericals): $$\Delta P_{total} = K_{m} \cdot v_{f} + K_{c} \cdot v_{f} \cdot W$$ **Step 5: Factor out the common term** For practical analysis, we factor out $v_f$: $$\Delta P_{total} = v_{f} \cdot (K_{m} + K_{c} \cdot W)$$ **Step 6: Engineering Interpretation** - When the filter is clean ($W = 0$), $\Delta P = K_m \cdot v_f$ (lowest pressure drop). - As dust accumulates ($W$ increases), $\Delta P$ rises linearly. - A **maximum allowable $\Delta P$** is set (typically **1500–2500 Pa**). When reached, the cleaning cycle is triggered. - This equation allows engineers to size the cleaning frequency and filter area. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deutsch-Anderson Equation for ESP Efficiency **Starting Principle:** The Deutsch-Anderson equation is the *most important equation* in ESP design and is almost always asked in KTU Module 3 derivations. It is derived from a **first-order mass balance** on a differential slice of the ESP. **Step 1: Set up the differential element** Consider a small slice of length $dx$ along the gas flow direction in a plate-type ESP. Let: - $Q$ = gas volumetric flow rate (m³/s) - $v$ = gas velocity (m/s) - $w$ = particle migration velocity toward plate (m/s) - $B$ = distance between plates (m) (also called $d_b$) - $N_p$ = number of uncollected particles per unit volume leaving the slice **Step 2: Define the rate of particle collection** The volumetric flow rate that reaches the collection plate within the slice is: $$dQ_{collect} = w \cdot \dfrac{dx}{B} \cdot Q$$ This represents the fraction of the gas volume that "sweeps" across the gap between the plates. **Step 3: Apply the mass balance** The rate of particle removal is proportional to the number concentration $N_p$: $$Q \cdot dN_p = -N_p \cdot w \cdot \dfrac{dx}{B}$$ **Step 4: Separate variables and integrate** Rearranging: $$\dfrac{dN_p}{N_p} = -\dfrac{w}{v \cdot B} \cdot dx$$ (Note: we used $v = Q/A_{cross-section} = Q/(B \cdot W_{plate})$, so $dx/v$ simplifies appropriately.) For a flat plate geometry, integrating from inlet ($x=0$, $N_p = N_p^0$) to outlet ($x=L$, $N_p = N_p$): $$\int_{N_{p}^{0}}^{N_{p}} \dfrac{dN_{p}}{N_{p}} = -\int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{w}{v \cdot B} \cdot dx$$ **Step 5: Evaluate the integrals** The left side gives $\ln(N_p / N_p^0)$ and the right side gives $-wL/(vB)$: $$\ln\left(\dfrac{N_{p}}{N_{p}^{0}}\right) = -\dfrac{w \cdot L}{v \cdot B}$$ **Step 6: Convert to collection efficiency** Efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the fraction of particles removed: $$\eta = 1 - \dfrac{N_{p}}{N_{p}^{0}}$$ Exponentiating: $$\dfrac{N_{p}}{N_{p}^{0}} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot L}{v \cdot B}\right)$$ **Step 7: Generalize to multi-field / total plate area** Note that $L/B$ for a single plate can be generalized to the **total collection area $A$ per unit gas flow $Q$**, called the **Specific Collection Area (SCA)**. Using the identity $A/Q = L/(v \cdot B)$ for plate geometry: $$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)}$$ This is the **Deutsch-Anderson equation** — the cornerstone of KTU ESP design questions. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (Analytical Matrix) ### **Example 1 (Fabric Filter — Air-to-Cloth Ratio)** **Problem:** A pulse-jet baghouse handles **12,000 m³/hr** of flue gas. The total filter cloth area installed is **500 m²**. Calculate the air-to-cloth ratio in **m/min** and comment on whether the design is suitable for a pulse-jet application. **Step 1: Convert gas flow rate** $$Q = 12{,}000 \ \text{m}^3/\text{hr} \times \dfrac{1 \ \text{hr}}{60 \ \text{min}} = 200 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 2: Apply the air-to-cloth ratio formula** $$A/C = \dfrac{Q}{A_{c}} = \dfrac{200 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}}{500 \ \text{m}^2} = 0.4 \ \text{m/min}$$ **Step 3: Comment on design suitability** The recommended A/C ratio for pulse-jet baghouses is **1.0 – 3.0 m/min**. A value of **0.4 m/min** is **too low** — this means the baghouse is **oversized**, leading to high capital cost. The fabric area could be reduced to **~70–200 m²** for optimum operation. --- ### **Example 2 (ESP — Deutsch-Anderson Efficiency)** **Problem:** An ESP has a total collection plate area of **4,000 m²** and handles a gas flow rate of **20 m³/s**. The migration velocity of the dust particles is **0.15 m/s**. Calculate the expected collection efficiency. **Step 1: Identify the formula (Deutsch-Anderson)** $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ **Step 2: Substitute the given values** $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{0.15 \times 4{,}000}{20}\right)$$ **Step 3: Calculate the exponent** $$-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q} = -\dfrac{600}{20} = -30$$ **Step 4: Evaluate the exponential** $$\exp(-30) \approx 9.36 \times 10^{-14} \quad (\text{negligibly small})$$ **Step 5: Final efficiency** $$\eta = 1 - 9.36 \times 10^{-14} \approx 0.99999... \approx 99.999\%$$ **Comment:** This is the theoretical maximum. Real-world ESPs achieve **~99%** due to non-ideal flow, re-entrainment, and rapping losses — a common KTU exam discussion point. --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **List the five mechanisms by which fabric filters capture particulate matter from a gas stream.** **Model Answer:** The five collection mechanisms in fabric filters are: 1. **Straining** — Particles larger than fabric pores are mechanically trapped. 2. **Inertial Impaction** — Large/dense particles deviate from gas streamlines and strike fibers. 3. **Interception** — Mid-sized particles following streamlines touch fibers as they pass nearby. 4. **Diffusion (Brownian Motion)** — Submicron particles move randomly and collide with fibers. 5. **Electrostatic Attraction** — Naturally charged particles or fibers attract oppositely charged particles. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** Listing all 5 mechanisms in correct order (largest to smallest particle) scores full marks. Skipping the electrostatic mechanism costs 1 mark. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **State the Deutsch-Anderson equation. What is its significance in ESP design?** **Model Answer:** The **Deutsch-Anderson equation** for ESP collection efficiency is: $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ where $w$ = migration velocity (m/s), $A$ = total collection plate area (m²), and $Q$ = gas flow rate (m³/s). **Significance:** - It relates ESP efficiency to the **migration velocity** and **Specific Collection Area (SCA = A/Q)**. - It is the **primary design equation** for sizing ESP plate areas for a target efficiency. - It is derived from a first-order mass balance on a differential element of the ESP. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a pulse-jet fabric filter. List its advantages over a reverse-air baghouse.** **Model Answer:** **Construction:** A pulse-jet baghouse consists of the following components: 1. **Filter Bags** — Long, cylindrical bags (typically 4–6 m long, 0.12–0.30 m diameter) suspended vertically from a tubesheet. 2. **Cages (Support Frames)** — Internal wire cages prevent bag collapse during filtration. 3. **Hopper** — Conical bottom section for collecting dislodged dust. 4. **Pulse Valves** — Solenoid-operated valves for injecting compressed air. 5. **Compressed Air Reservoir** — High-pressure air source (typically 5–7 bar). 6. **Venturi Nozzle** — Mounted at the top of each bag to amplify cleaning pulse. **Working:** - Dirty gas enters the hopper section, flows **outside-to-inside** through the bag fabric, and clean gas exits through the top. - A **dust cake** forms on the bag surface; pressure drop rises. - When ΔP reaches a critical value, the controller triggers the **solenoid pulse valve**. - A short burst (0.1 s) of **compressed air** is injected into the bag, creating a shock wave. - The bag inflates and snaps, dislodging the dust cake. - Dust falls into the hopper and is removed via a **rotary airlock**. **Advantages over Reverse-Air Baghouse:** 1. Operates continuously (no need to take bags offline for cleaning). 2. Higher air-to-cloth ratio (1.0–3.0 m/min vs 0.5–1.0 m/min). 3. Smaller footprint and lower capital cost for the same duty. 4. Higher cleaning intensity → better for sticky or fine dust. 5. Faster cleaning cycle (frequent, brief pulses vs long reverse-air cycles). **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **A baghouse handles 25,000 m³/hr of gas at 150 °C. The filter media is polyester with $K_m$ = 80 Pa·s/m and cake specific resistance $K_c$ = 1.5 × 10⁸ m/kg. The filtration velocity is 1.2 m/min. The dust mass per unit area after one cycle is 0.4 kg/m². Calculate:** 1. The total pressure drop. 2. The required filter cloth area. 3. The number of bags if each bag has an area of 2.5 m². **Model Solution:** **Part (b)(i) — Total Pressure Drop:** **Step 1:** Convert velocity to SI units. $$v_f = 1.2 \ \text{m/min} = \dfrac{1.2}{60} = 0.02 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the pressure drop equation. $$\Delta P = K_m \cdot v_f + K_c \cdot v_f \cdot W$$ $$\Delta P = (80)(0.02) + (1.5 \times 10^{8})(0.02)(0.4)$$ **Step 3:** Calculate each term. - Medium term: $80 \times 0.02 = 1.6 \ \text{Pa}$ - Cake term: $1.5 \times 10^{8} \times 0.02 \times 0.4 = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Pa}$ **Step 4:** Sum the terms. $$\Delta P = 1.6 + 1{,}200{,}000 = 1{,}200{,}001.6 \ \text{Pa} \approx 1.2 \ \text{MPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Most students forget to convert **m/min to m/s**. KTU examiners award 1 mark for correct unit conversion alone. Without it, the final answer is **50× off**! **Part (b)(ii) — Required Filter Cloth Area:** **Step 1:** Convert gas flow to m³/min. $$Q = \dfrac{25{,}000}{60} = 416.67 \ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the filtration velocity relation. $$A_c = \dfrac{Q}{v_f} = \dfrac{416.67}{1.2} = 347.22 \ \text{m}^2$$ **Part (b)(iii) — Number of Bags:** $$N = \dfrac{A_c}{A_{bag}} = \dfrac{347.22}{2.5} \approx 139 \ \text{bags}$$ Always round **up to the next whole number** to provide design margin → **140 bags**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Explain the working of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) with a neat block diagram. What is corona discharge? How does it affect ESP efficiency?** **Model Answer:** **Working of ESP (4 stages):** 1. **Corona Discharge Generation:** A high negative DC voltage (**30–100 kV**) is applied between thin discharge wires and grounded collection plates. The strong non-uniform electric field near the wires ionizes surrounding gas, producing a bluish glow (corona). 2. **Particle Charging:** Dust particles entering the ESP collide with negative gas ions and acquire a charge (bombardment/diffusion charging). 3. **Particle Migration:** Charged particles experience a force $F = qE$ toward the positively charged collection plate. The drift velocity is called **migration velocity (w)**. 4. **Collection and Removal:** Particles deposit on the plate. Periodic **rapping** dislodges the dust layer into the hopper. **Corona Discharge:** Corona discharge is a localized electrical discharge that occurs when the electric field strength near a high-voltage electrode (wire) is high enough to ionize surrounding gas molecules (typically air), creating a region of charged ions and free electrons. It appears as a faint bluish glow and hissing sound. **Effect on ESP Efficiency:** - A **stable corona** ensures consistent particle charging → high efficiency. - **Excessive voltage** causes **back corona** (if dust resistivity is high), discharging collected particles and dropping efficiency. - **Low voltage** = weak charging = low efficiency. An optimal operating voltage exists (typically **30–50 kV** for industrial ESPs). **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **A plate-type ESP handles 30 m³/s of flue gas. The plate area installed is 5,000 m². The migration velocity is 0.12 m/s. Calculate:** 1. The Deutsch-Anderson efficiency. 2. The required plate area to achieve 99.9% efficiency. 3. Comment on the design implications if the gas flow is doubled to 60 m³/s. **Model Solution:** **Part (b)(i) — Deutsch-Anderson Efficiency:** **Step 1:** Apply the Deutsch-Anderson equation. $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute values. $$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{0.12 \times 5{,}000}{30}\right) = 1 - \exp(-20)$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate. $$\exp(-20) \approx 2.06 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\eta \approx 1 - 2.06 \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.999999998 \approx 99.9999\%$$ **Part (b)(ii) — Plate Area for 99.9% Efficiency:** **Step 1:** Rearrange the Deutsch-Anderson equation to solve for $A$. $$1 - \eta = \exp\left(-\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}\right)$$ $$\ln(1 - \eta) = -\dfrac{w \cdot A}{Q}$$ $$A = -\dfrac{Q}{w} \cdot \ln(1 - \eta)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $\eta = 0.999$, $Q = 30$ m³/s, $w = 0.12$ m/s. $$1 - \eta = 0.001$$ $$\ln(0.001) = -6.9078$$ **Step 3:** Calculate. $$A = -\dfrac{30}{0.12} \times (-6.9078) = 250 \times 6.9078 = 1{,}726.95 \ \text{m}^2$$ **Answer:** Approximately **1,727 m²** of plate area is required (much less than the 5,000 m² installed — the ESP is overdesigned for the original load). **Part (b)(iii) — Design Implication for Doubled Flow:** **Step 1:** Calculate the new efficiency with $Q = 60$ m³/s (same $A$ and $w$). $$\eta_{new} = 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{0.12 \times 5{,}000}{60}\right) = 1 - \exp(-10)$$ $$\eta_{new} = 1 - 4.54 \times 10^{-5} \approx 99.995\%$$ **Comment:** Doubling the gas flow **decreases efficiency** (from 99.9999% to 99.995%) because the SCA (A/Q) is halved. To maintain 99.9% efficiency at 60 m³/s, the required plate area would scale linearly: $A_{new} = 2 \times 1{,}727 \approx 3{,}454 \ \text{m}^2$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (ESP):** > - **Forgetting the negative sign** in the rearranged equation: $A = -(Q/w) \cdot \ln(1-\eta)$ — losing this gives a **negative area**, a guaranteed zero. > - **Confusing A/C ratio with SCA** — fabric filter uses $Q/A_c$ (m/min), ESP uses $A/Q$ (s/m). These are **inverses with different units**. > - **Not commenting** on the design implication part in (iii) costs **2 marks** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the practical insight. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Exam Checklist (Module 3 — Fabric Filters & ESP):** ### **Fabric Filters (Baghouses)** - ☐ Definition: Dry device using **porous fabric** to filter particulates from gas streams. - ☐ **5 collection mechanisms**: Straining, Impaction, Interception, Diffusion, Electrostatic. - ☐ **3 types**: Shaker, Reverse-Air, Pulse-Jet (know cleaning method for each). - ☐ **Key equation**: $\Delta P = K_m \cdot v_f + K_c \cdot v_f \cdot W$ — memorize!
